notes.note: Cicero fears renewed war in Gaul, where the Caesarian legions and ambitious commanders (Lepidus, Plancus, Pollio, and others) controlled the provinces; the legions there were the great unknown in the months after the assassination.

notes.anchor: and where Sextus himself will come out
notes.note: Sextus Pompeius, Pompey's surviving son, still under arms in Spain in 44 BC; Cicero anxiously watches which way his considerable forces will turn.

notes.anchor: the Ides of March are a consolation
notes.note: "The Ides of March" stands for the killing of Caesar on 15 March 44 BC; even amid his fears Cicero takes comfort that the deed was done.

Greek heroes, "heroes"; Cicero's admiring collective name for the assassins (Brutus, Cassius, and their fellow "Liberators"), praising the deed itself while lamenting that they made no plan for what came after. Refers to a group, so left unbound to a single entity.
